mappings.rationaleAI: Comprehensive coverage of formal logic including propositional and first-order classical logic (A.1, A.2), syllogistic reasoning (A.3), modal and deviant logics (A.4), metalogic and formal semantics (B), and fallacies and argumentation (C.1).

Incompleteness theorems directly address completeness, consistency, and decidability of formal mathematical systems (B.3); independence results for axiom of choice and continuum hypothesis relate to set theory and natural-number arithmetic (A.5); proof theory contributions fall within metalogic broadly (B).

ZFC axiomatic set theory, cardinality of infinite sets, and foundational role underpinning mathematical structures directly correspond to set theory and natural-number arithmetic within formal logic (A.5).
AI employs formal logic as a core technique for reasoning and decision-making, constituting a physical and computational application of logic (C.4).

Proof methods (direct, induction, contradiction) operate within formal logical systems (A); formal proofs using symbolic logic connect to formal systems and formal languages (B.2); proof theory intersects metalogic (B).
Aristotle founded formal logic through the Organon and the theory of syllogistic reasoning (A.3); his work on demonstration and fallacies connects to the critique of forms of reasoning (C.1).

Semantic theories of truth using metalanguage to analyze object-language truth conditions are central to metalogic (B); the liar paradox and truth-value gaps connect to formal semantics; epistemic dimensions of truth relate to epistemic logic (C.2).

Deductive, inductive, abductive, and analogical reasoning constitute forms of applied reasoning (C); systematic biases and fallacies documented by cognitive science connect to analysis of logical fallacies (C.1.b).

Formal ontology applied in knowledge representation uses logical frameworks; Quine's criterion of ontological commitment connects logic to ontological questions within model theory and formal semantics (B.5).

Metaphysical questions about necessary versus contingent truths and modality intersect with epistemic and modal logic (C.2); the connection to logic is tangential but present through formal treatments of necessity.
Epistemology examines knowledge, belief, justification, and doubt -- the core concepts of epistemic logic dealing with belief, knowledge, assertion, doubt, and question (C.2).

The Turing machine formalized computability and decidability, directly addressing decidability and undecidability within metalogic (B.3); the Turing test and AI contributions represent logical application to physical systems (C.4).

Propositional variables are fundamental elements of the propositional calculus (A.1.a); bound and free variables in mathematical logic are central to the predicate calculus with individual and predicate variables (A.2.a).
Peano axioms and set-theoretic construction of natural numbers directly relate to set theory and natural-number arithmetic within formal logic (A.5).

Cantor's results on infinite cardinalities and ZFC axiom of infinity relate to set theory and natural-number arithmetic (A.5); connections to computability and logical limits touch on metalogical discoveries about formal systems (B.3).
Laws of thought (identity, non-contradiction, excluded middle) and critical thinking relate to the critique of forms of reasoning and analysis of logical fallacies (C.1).
Inductive hypothesis development, deductive prediction, and falsifiability as a demarcation criterion represent forms of reasoning analyzed within applied logic (C.1).
Cryptographic systems rest on computational hardness assumptions and formal logical structures, representing a physical and computational application of logic (C.4).
Information theory's formal quantification of information in bits and its connection to encoding and pattern recognition constitute a logical application to physical and computational systems (C.4).
Mathematical logic and set theory as branches of mathematics connect to set theory and natural-number arithmetic (A.5); the foundational crisis and axiomatic method relate to metalogic (B).
